title.description: An optional heading for the whole procedure (e.g., 'Installation', 'Build your first component', 'Submitting a contribution').

ordered.description: Whether the steps must be followed in order. Defaults to true (a sequential procedure). Set false for an unordered checklist where steps are independent. Tools SHOULD render ordered procedures as numbered lists and unordered ones as a checklist or bulleted list.

items.description: The steps, in display order. Each item is a single step with a title and optional instruction, examples, and expected result. Ordering is significant, so tools MUST preserve it for ordered procedures.

variantValue.description: A single option within an enum variant dimension.

identifier.description: Machine-readable value identifier (e.g., 'sm', 'md', 'lg', 'primary', 'secondary').

description.description: What this value looks like, when to use it, and how it differs from other values in this dimension.

rationale.description: Why this value exists: the user need or design rationale it addresses (e.g., 'High emphasis directs the user to the single most important action on the surface'). For comparative when-to-use guidance across values, use the entity's useCases block instead.

examples.description: Examples showing this variant value in isolation or in context.

tokens.description: Design token overrides applied when this value is selected. Keys are token-purpose names (e.g., 'background', 'text-color', 'padding-horizontal'). Values are token identifiers. In a system with a token layer these MUST be token identifiers, not raw values, so the value can't silently drift from the token source.

flagVariant.description: A boolean toggle variant that is either on or off. Use it for binary capabilities such as 'disabled', 'full-width', 'icon-only', or 'loading'. Flag variants have no values array. The component either has the flag active or not.

kind.description: Identifies this variant as a boolean flag.

identifier.description: Machine-readable identifier of the flag (e.g., 'disabled', 'full-width', 'icon-only', 'loading').

name.description: Human-readable name of the flag (e.g., 'Disabled', 'Full Width', 'Icon Only', 'Loading').

description.description: What this flag controls and how it affects the component's appearance or behavior when active.

rationale.description: Why this flag exists: the user need or design rationale it addresses (e.g., 'Prevents user interaction during form submission to avoid duplicate requests').

examples.description: Examples showing the component with this flag active.

tokens.description: Design token overrides applied when this flag is active. Keys are token-purpose names. Values are token identifiers. In a system with a token layer these MUST be token identifiers, not raw values, so the value can't silently drift from the token source.

enumVariant.description: An enumerated variant with multiple mutually exclusive values. Use it for dimensions where the user picks one option from a set, such as 'size' (sm | md | lg), 'emphasis' (primary | secondary | ghost), or 'shape' (rounded | pill | square).

kind.description: Identifies this variant as an enumerated set of values.

identifier.description: Machine-readable identifier of the variant dimension (e.g., 'size', 'emphasis', 'shape'). This is the axis name, not a value within it.

name.description: Human-readable name of the variant dimension (e.g., 'Size', 'Emphasis', 'Shape').

description.description: What this dimension of variation controls and how its values affect the component's appearance or behavior.

values.description: The possible values for this variant. A single entry is valid — real systems carry single-value dimensions mid-migration (a new dimension introduced one value at a time). Ordering is significant, so tools SHOULD keep it for display.

variants.description: Documents all dimensions of visual or behavioral variation on a component. Each item is either a flag variant (a boolean toggle like 'disabled' or 'full-width') or an enum variant (a set of mutually exclusive options like size: sm | md | lg). Multiple items document orthogonal dimensions that combine independently.

kind.description: Identifies this document block as a variants spec.

items.description: The variant dimensions. Each item is either a flag (boolean toggle) or an enum (set of values). Ordering is significant, so tools SHOULD display dimensions in this order.

chunk.description: A pre-composed block of code that captures a design system pattern. Chunks give consumers a working starting point assembled from system components — for example, a layout, a settings form, or a confirmation dialog. The top level holds its identity (`identifier`, `name`, optional `description`), the `code` block that consumers copy, `guidelines` for how to use and adapt it, and `useCases` for when it is the right choice. Chunks are intentionally simple: they document a code composition, not the full anatomy/API surface a component carries.

kind.description: Identifies this entity as a chunk.

identifier.description: Machine-readable identifier for the chunk (e.g., 'search-bar', 'settings-form', 'confirmation-dialog'). MUST be lowercase kebab-case and unique within its entity group.

name.description: Display name shown in docs (e.g., 'Search bar', 'Settings form', 'Confirmation dialog').

description.description: What this chunk is, the pattern it captures, and which components it composes. CommonMark supported.

code.description: The pre-composed block of code consumers copy to use the chunk. Provide it in exactly one of two forms: inline (`code` + `language`) when the source travels with the document, or referenced (`src` + `language`) when it lives in an external source file. The referenced form keeps long sources readable and lets a chunk share one canonical file with a live app. Consumers resolve `src` at read time — no build step inlines it.

oneOf.title: Inline code
oneOf.description: The source travels inside the document as a string.

code.description: The source code of the chunk — the block consumers copy and adapt.

language.description: The programming language or syntax of the code (e.g., 'jsx', 'tsx', 'html', 'css', 'vue', 'svelte').

oneOf.title: Referenced code
oneOf.description: The source lives in an external file the consumer resolves at read time.

src.description: A relative path to the source file that holds the chunk's code (e.g., './chunks/confirmation-dialog.tsx'). MUST be relative. Absolute paths, protocol-relative references, and scheme-bearing URIs (http:, file:, …) are not permitted. Resolvers SHOULD gate any remote dereferencing behind an allow-list, following the same rules a `$ref` fileRef obeys. The file is read verbatim — no build step inlines it into the document.

language.description: The programming language or syntax of the referenced file (e.g., 'jsx', 'tsx', 'html', 'css', 'vue', 'svelte').

guidelines.description: Actionable rules for using and adapting the chunk. Each item pairs a guidance statement with a rationale and an RFC 2119 enforcement level. Reuses the shared guideline-entry shape so chunk rules read the same as guidelines elsewhere in the system.

useCases.description: Scenario-driven use cases describing when this chunk is — or is not — the right choice. Each entry is marked 'recommended' or 'discouraged'. Reuses the shared use-case shape. Authors SHOULD lead with recommended scenarios.

relationships.description: Typed dependency edges from this chunk to other entities — chiefly 'composes' the components it is built from (mark `required` true for essential ones). The structural counterpart to listing components in `links`; the graph agents read for impact and alternatives. Inverse edges are derived by tooling.

metadata.description: Optional metadata fields, keyed by name: status, since, lastUpdated, category, tags, aliases, summary, thumbnail, preview, extends, and links. Use the `links` field to point at the components this chunk composes.

component.description: A single reusable UI component. The top level holds its identity: `identifier`, `name`, and `description`. The `metadata` object holds optional fields: status, since, lastUpdated, category, tags, aliases, summary, thumbnail, preview, extends, and links. The `documentBlocks` array holds structured docs as typed entries: imports, anatomy, api, variants, states, design-specifications, plus the general kinds.

kind.description: Identifies this entity as a component.

identifier.description: Machine-readable identifier (e.g., 'button', 'dialog', 'form-field', 'data-table'). MUST be unique within its entity group.

name.description: Display name shown in docs (e.g., 'Button', 'Dialog', 'Form Field', 'Data Table').

description.description: What this component is, what it does, and its role in the design system. CommonMark supported.

metadata.description: Optional metadata fields, keyed by name: status, since, lastUpdated, category, tags, aliases, summary, thumbnail, preview, extends, and links.

relationships.description: Typed dependency edges from this component to other entities — e.g. 'depends-on' a token, 'composes' another component, 'alternative-to' a sibling. The structural graph agents read for impact and alternatives. Inverse edges are derived by tooling. Use the `links` metadata field for external resources.

documentBlocks.description: All structured docs for this component, as an ordered array of typed document block objects. It accepts the component-specific kinds (imports, anatomy, api, variants, states, design-specifications) plus the general kinds (guidelines, useCases, accessibility, content, sections). The `kind` property on each entry sets its shape. Order matters, so tools SHOULD keep it for display.

agentDocumentBlocks.description: Structured docs for agents (AI/LLM) only. This is a separate space that accepts the same document block kinds as `documentBlocks`. Use it for guidance that would be noise for human readers: generation limits, ways to tell confusable entities apart, and machine-checkable rules with evidence. Tools MUST NOT render these blocks in human-facing docs. Agents SHOULD read both arrays — human-facing blocks first, then these.

foundation.description: The principles, guidelines, scales, and token references that govern a broad design domain across the system, such as color, typography, spacing, elevation, motion, accessibility, and content. Foundations are wider than a single visual attribute. A domain may be visual (color, spacing) or cross-cutting (accessibility, content). The top level holds its identity: `identifier`, `name`, and `description`. The `metadata` object holds optional fields: status, since, lastUpdated, category, tags, aliases, summary, thumbnail, preview, extends, and links. The `documentBlocks` array holds structured docs as typed entries: principles, scale, motion, guidelines, accessibility, content, and so on.

kind.description: Identifies this entity as a foundation.

identifier.description: Machine-readable identifier for the foundation (e.g., 'color', 'typography', 'spacing', 'elevation', 'motion', 'shape', 'accessibility', 'content'). MUST be unique within its entity group.

description.description: What this foundation covers, the design domain it governs, and its role in the system. CommonMark supported.

metadata.description: Optional metadata fields, keyed by name: status, since, lastUpdated, category, tags, aliases, summary, thumbnail, preview, extends, and links.

relationships.description: Typed dependency edges from this foundation to other entities — e.g. 'depends-on' the tokens it builds on. Inverse edges are derived by tooling. Use the `links` metadata field for external resources.

documentBlocks.description: All structured docs for this foundation, as an ordered array of typed document block objects. It accepts the foundation-specific kinds (principles, scale, motion) plus the general kinds (guidelines, useCases, accessibility, content, sections). The `kind` property on each entry sets its shape. Order matters, so tools SHOULD keep it for display. Put internal artifact links (such as token group references) in the `links` metadata field, not in document block entries.

agentDocumentBlocks.description: Structured docs for agents (AI/LLM) only. This is a separate space that accepts the same document block kinds as `documentBlocks`. Use it for guidance that would be noise for human readers: generation limits, ways to tell confusable entities apart, and machine-checkable rules with evidence. Tools MUST NOT render these blocks in human-facing docs. Agents SHOULD read both arrays — human-facing blocks first, then these.

guide.description: A long-form, reading-oriented document: a getting-started walkthrough, a contribution guide, a tutorial, a conceptual overview, a migration guide, or any narrative guidance the system needs. Guides fill the gap left by the artifact-bound entities (component, token, foundation, pattern). Those entities document a single thing, not a journey or a concept. The top level holds its identity: `identifier`, `name`, and `description`. The `metadata` object holds optional fields. Use `category` (e.g., 'getting-started', 'contribution', 'tutorial', 'concept', 'migration') to classify the guide, and `tags` for finer-grained discovery. The `documentBlocks` array holds structured docs as typed entries: sections (narrative prose), steps (procedures), guidelines, useCases, imports, accessibility, and content.

kind.description: Identifies this entity as a guide.

identifier.description: Machine-readable identifier for the guide (e.g., 'getting-started', 'contributing', 'theming-tutorial', 'migrating-to-v2'). MUST be lowercase kebab-case and unique within its entity group.

name.description: Display name shown in docs (e.g., 'Getting started', 'Contributing', 'Theming tutorial', 'Migrating to v2').

description.description: What this guide covers, who it is for, and what the reader will accomplish. CommonMark supported.

metadata.description: Optional metadata fields, keyed by name: status, since, lastUpdated, category, tags, aliases, summary, thumbnail, preview, extends, and links. Use `category` to classify the guide (e.g., 'getting-started', 'contribution', 'tutorial', 'concept', 'migration').

relationships.description: Typed dependency edges from this guide to the entities it documents or depends on. Inverse edges are derived by tooling. Use the `links` metadata field for external resources.

documentBlocks.description: All structured docs for this guide, as an ordered array of typed document block objects. It accepts the guide-specific kinds (sections, steps) plus reused kinds (imports for setup instructions, guidelines, useCases, accessibility, content). The `kind` property on each entry sets its shape. A guide reads top to bottom, so order matters and tools SHOULD keep it for display. Put links to other entities and external resources in the `links` metadata field or a section's `links`, not in document block entries.

agentDocumentBlocks.description: Structured docs for agents (AI/LLM) only. This is a separate space that accepts the same document block kinds as `documentBlocks`. Use it for guidance that would be noise for human readers: generation limits, ways to tell confusable entities apart, and machine-checkable rules with evidence. Tools MUST NOT render these blocks in human-facing docs. Agents SHOULD read both arrays — human-facing blocks first, then these.

pattern.description: A repeated, multi-component solution to a common UX problem. Patterns sit above individual components. They show how those components combine to meet a user need. The top level holds its identity: `identifier`, `name`, and `description`. The `metadata` object holds optional fields: status, since, lastUpdated, category, tags, aliases, summary, thumbnail, preview, extends, and links. The `documentBlock` array holds structured docs as typed entries: `interactions` for flow steps, `guidelines` for rules, `accessibility` for a11y specs, and so on.

kind.description: Identifies this entity as a pattern.

identifier.description: Machine-readable identifier for the pattern (e.g., 'error-messaging', 'navigation', 'empty-state', 'form-validation'). MUST be unique within its entity group.

name.description: Display name shown in docs (e.g., 'Error Messaging', 'Navigation', 'Empty State').

description.description: What this pattern is, the UX problem it solves, and how its components compose to meet a user need. CommonMark supported.

metadata.description: Optional metadata fields, keyed by name: status, since, lastUpdated, category, tags, aliases, summary, thumbnail, preview, extends, and links.

relationships.description: Typed dependency edges from this pattern to other entities — chiefly 'composes' the components it is built from (mark `required` true for essential ones), plus 'depends-on' and 'alternative-to'. The structural counterpart to listing components in `links`. Inverse edges are derived by tooling.

documentBlocks.description: All structured docs for this pattern, as an ordered array of typed document block objects. Patterns accept the pattern-specific kinds (interactions) plus the general kinds (guidelines, useCases, accessibility, content, sections). The `kind` property on each entry sets its shape. Order matters, so tools SHOULD keep it for display. Interaction entries rely on order to show the flow over time. List the components a pattern uses in the `links` metadata field (kind 'component', name, and role), not in a dedicated block kind.

agentDocumentBlocks.description: Structured docs for agents (AI/LLM) only. This is a separate space that accepts the same document block kinds as `documentBlocks`. Use it for guidance that would be noise for human readers: generation limits, ways to tell confusable entities apart, and machine-checkable rules with evidence. Tools MUST NOT render these blocks in human-facing docs. Agents SHOULD read both arrays — human-facing blocks first, then these.

tokenOverride.description: A single token override inside a theme: which token changes, and why. The override value lives in the DTCG file. This entry records the intent and reason.

token.description: The identifier of the token being overridden. MUST match the `identifier` of a token defined elsewhere in the system. Token identifiers follow no fixed pattern, so they can support DTCG and design tool naming styles (e.g., 'color-text-primary', 'color.text.primary', 'color/text/primary').

description.description: Says what changes for this token in this theme and why (e.g., 'Lightened to meet WCAG AAA contrast on the dark background'). The resolved value lives in the DTCG file.

theme.description: A named context that adapts the design system's token values. DSDS captures the purpose, usage document blocks, accessibility notes, and the list of affected tokens. The override values live in the DTCG file that `source` points to. Use themes for color modes (light, dark, high-contrast), density settings (compact, comfortable), brand variants, or product-specific tweaks. The token catalog stays the source of truth for token identity, type, and docs. The `metadata` object holds optional fields: status, since, lastUpdated, category, tags, aliases, summary, thumbnail, preview, extends, and links.

kind.description: Identifies this entity as a theme.

identifier.description: Machine-readable identifier for the theme (e.g., 'dark', 'high-contrast', 'compact', 'brand-sub-brand').

name.description: Display name for the theme (e.g., 'Dark Mode', 'High Contrast', 'Compact Density').

description.description: What this theme is for, the context it adapts the system to, and when to apply it. CommonMark supported.

metadata.description: Optional metadata fields, keyed by name: status, since, lastUpdated, category, tags, aliases, summary, thumbnail, preview, extends, and links.

source.description: Points to the W3C Design Tokens (DTCG) definition. DSDS does not copy token values. The DTCG file is the source of truth for resolved values, aliases, and type info.

file.description: URI or relative path to the DTCG file containing this theme's token override definitions.

path.description: The dot-path to this theme's group within the DTCG file, if applicable.

overrides.description: Token overrides this theme applies. Each entry says which token changes and why. List only tokens whose values differ from the default. Unlisted tokens keep their default values. The override values live in the DTCG file that `source` points to.

relationships.description: Typed dependency edges from this theme to other entities — e.g. 'depends-on' the tokens it overrides, 'extends' a base theme. Inverse edges are derived by tooling.

documentBlocks.description: All structured docs for this theme, as an ordered array of typed document block objects. Themes accept the general kinds only (guidelines, useCases, accessibility, content, sections). The `kind` property on each entry sets its shape. Order matters, so tools SHOULD keep it for display.

agentDocumentBlocks.description: Structured docs for agents (AI/LLM) only. This is a separate space that accepts the same document block kinds as `documentBlocks`. Use it for guidance that would be noise for human readers: generation limits, ways to tell confusable entities apart, and machine-checkable rules with evidence. Tools MUST NOT render these blocks in human-facing docs. Agents SHOULD read both arrays — human-facing blocks first, then these.

token.description: Docs for a single design token: identifier, type, use cases, guidelines, and accessibility notes. The display `name` property is absent. The `identifier` itself serves as the display label. Description and status are optional. The `metadata` object holds optional fields: status, since, lastUpdated, category, tags, aliases, summary, thumbnail, preview, extends, and links. When a token sits inside a token group and omits its own status field, the parent group's status applies.

kind.description: Identifies this entity as a token.

identifier.description: The token identifier. It follows no fixed pattern, so it can support DTCG and design tool naming styles that use dots, slashes, or other separators (e.g., 'color-text-primary', 'color.text.primary', 'color/text/primary'). It also serves as the human-readable display label.

name.description: Optional display name (e.g., 'Primary Text'). When omitted, the `identifier` serves as the display label — the uniform rule for all entities is: display `name` when present, otherwise `identifier`.

tokenType.description: The type of token aligned with the DTCG spec (e.g., 'color', 'dimension', 'fontFamily', 'fontWeight', 'duration', 'cubicBezier', 'shadow').

description.description: What this token represents and when to use it. Optional, so keep it terse at scale. CommonMark supported.

source.description: Points to the W3C Design Tokens (DTCG) definition. DSDS does not copy token values. The DTCG file is the source of truth for resolved values, aliases, and type info.

file.description: URI or relative path to the DTCG file containing this token's definition.

path.description: The dot-path to this token within the DTCG file (e.g., 'color.text.primary').

metadata.description: Optional metadata fields, keyed by name: status, since, lastUpdated, category, tags, aliases, summary, thumbnail, preview, extends, and links.

relationships.description: Typed dependency edges to other entities — e.g. 'depends-on' a token this one aliases, 'part-of' a token group. Inverse edges are derived by tooling.

documentBlocks.description: All structured docs for this token, as an ordered array of typed document block objects. Tokens accept the general kinds only (guidelines, useCases, accessibility, content, sections). The `kind` property on each entry sets its shape. Order matters, so tools SHOULD keep it for display.

agentDocumentBlocks.description: Structured docs for agents (AI/LLM) only. This is a separate space that accepts the same document block kinds as `documentBlocks`. Use it for guidance that would be noise for human readers: generation limits, ways to tell confusable entities apart, and machine-checkable rules with evidence. Tools MUST NOT render these blocks in human-facing docs. Agents SHOULD read both arrays — human-facing blocks first, then these.

tokenGroup.description: A group of related design tokens. Token groups are flexible units: a full collection, a family (e.g., all color tokens), a sub-family (e.g., a single hue with its grade scale), or any other logical grouping. Groups can nest to form a deep hierarchy. A top-level group might stand for 'color' and hold child groups for 'color-text', 'color-background', and 'color-border', each with its own tokens. This nesting mirrors how real design systems organize tokens into families and sub-families. The display `name` property is absent. The `identifier` itself serves as the display label. Description and status are optional. The `metadata` object holds optional fields: status, since, lastUpdated, category, tags, aliases, summary, thumbnail, preview, and links. When a child omits its own status field, the parent group's status applies.

kind.description: Identifies this entity as a token-group.

identifier.description: The token group identifier. It follows no fixed pattern, so it can support DTCG and design tool naming styles (e.g., 'color-text', 'color.text', 'color/text'). It also serves as the human-readable display label.

name.description: Optional display name (e.g., 'Primary Text'). When omitted, the `identifier` serves as the display label — the uniform rule for all entities is: display `name` when present, otherwise `identifier`.

description.description: What this token group covers and how it is organized. Optional, so keep it terse at scale. CommonMark supported.

tokenType.description: When all tokens in this group share the same DTCG token type, declare it here so you don't repeat it on each child. Each token in children MAY override this value. Common values: 'color', 'dimension', 'fontFamily', 'fontWeight', 'duration', 'number', 'shadow'.

source.description: Points to the W3C Design Tokens (DTCG) definition. DSDS does not copy token values. The DTCG file is the source of truth for resolved values, aliases, and type info.

file.description: URI or relative path to the DTCG file containing this group's definition.

path.description: The dot-path to this group within the DTCG file (e.g., 'color.text').

children.description: An ordered array of tokens and/or nested token groups inside this group. Each item MUST be either a token or a tokenGroup. Order often shows a meaningful progression (e.g., lightest to darkest, or smallest to largest), so it matters and tools SHOULD keep it for display. Children inherit tokenType and status from this group when they omit their own values.

metadata.description: Optional metadata fields, keyed by name: status, since, lastUpdated, category, tags, aliases, summary, thumbnail, preview, extends, and links.

relationships.description: Typed dependency edges to other entities — e.g. 'depends-on' a token this one aliases, 'part-of' a token group. Inverse edges are derived by tooling.

documentBlocks.description: All structured docs for this token group, as an ordered array of typed document block objects. It accepts the general kinds only (guidelines, useCases, accessibility, content, sections). Use this array for docs that apply to the group as a whole. Put docs for individual tokens on the token inside `children`.

agentDocumentBlocks.description: Structured docs for agents (AI/LLM) only. This is a separate space that accepts the same document block kinds as `documentBlocks`. Use it for guidance that would be noise for human readers: generation limits, ways to tell confusable entities apart, and machine-checkable rules with evidence. Tools MUST NOT render these blocks in human-facing docs. Agents SHOULD read both arrays — human-facing blocks first, then these.

anyEntity.description: Any DSDS entity — component, token, token group, theme, foundation, pattern, guide, or chunk. The required `kind` property identifies the entity type and selects its schema. This is the single entity union. The root `entity` property and each entity group's `entities` array both accept it.

fileRef.description: A JSON Reference to a DSDS entity or entity group defined in another file. Tooling (e.g. json-schema-ref-parser) resolves the reference before validation and merge. The URI should include a JSON Pointer fragment to target a specific value within the referenced file (e.g. './button.dsds.json#/entity', './groups/tokens.dsds.json#/entityGroups/0').

$ref.description: A relative reference to a DSDS file, optionally with a JSON Pointer fragment that identifies the target value within that file (e.g., './button.dsds.json#/entity'). MUST be relative. Absolute paths, protocol-relative references, and scheme-bearing URIs (http:, file:, …) are not permitted. A reference to another system uses `extends.url`. Resolvers MUST detect cycles. They MUST treat unresolvable files or pointers and shape mismatches as fatal. They MUST gate any remote dereferencing behind an allow-list (see the spec's reference-resolution and security rules).

entityGroup.description: A named collection of design system entities. All entities live in a single `entities` array, in display order, mixing entity types freely. Each entity's required `kind` identifies its type, so no per-type arrays are needed. Only the group name is required.

name.description: Human-readable name of the collection (e.g., 'Acme Design System', 'Color Tokens', 'Button Documentation'). This is already a display label — it does NOT have an `identifier` counterpart and need not match a slug pattern.

entities.description: The entities in this group, in display order. Any mix of entity kinds — components, tokens, token groups, themes, foundations, patterns, guides, and chunks. Each item's `kind` identifies its type. Items may be inlined or referenced through a $ref object that points to another DSDS file. Order is significant: tools SHOULD keep it for display.
